personnel_count.notes: Single agency head

responsibilities: Guard against extraordinary incidents such as fire and flood outside the palace
Patrol the perimeter outside the palace three times a month
Manage weapons and armaments
historical_notes: The term 'Wu' (吾) is historically synonymous with 'Yu' (禦 - to ward off). Originally oversaw the Shidao Hou and Left/Right Zhonghou, who cleared the road for imperial processions; during the Eastern Han restoration (中興), these duties were made temporary assignments for Lang officers and detached from this office. Subordinate positions of Zhonglei, Sihu, Duchuan Ling, Cheng, Wei, and Left/Right Jingfu Duwei were abolished.

title_en: Assistant to the Bearer of the Gilded Mace
responsibilities: Assist the Bearer of the Gilded Mace in administrative and patrol duties
historical_notes: Standard administrative assistant per Han statutory salary scaling.

personnel_count.notes: No official salary rank (無秩); compensation is scaled comparable to standard sub-official functionaries (比吏食奉)

responsibilities: Provide armed escort and patrol support for the Bearer of the Gilded Mace
historical_notes: Retained as the primary physical force for capital policing outside the palace walls.

personnel_count.notes: Subordinate Assistant (丞) established at 300 Shi per meta-rules

responsibilities: Manage weapons and armaments
historical_notes: Transferred under the direct executive control of the Bearer of the Gilded Mace.

title_en: Grand Tutor to the Crown Prince

personnel_count.notes: Does not govern official subordinates (不領官屬)

responsibilities: Provide moral guidance, instruction, and counsel to the Crown Prince
Maintain ceremonial standing equivalent to an imperial teacher
historical_notes: Operates purely as an academic and moral dignitary without an administrative bureau.

personnel_count.notes: Post staffed exclusively by eunuchs during the Eastern Han

responsibilities: Convey and promulgate edicts and orders from the Empress (中宮)
Manage communications and audience requests between the throne and imperial consort kin
Escort the Empress on all formal excursions outside her quarters
historical_notes: Origins in the Qin Dynasty post of Jiangxing (將行), staffed by eunuchs. Emperor Jing renamed it Dachangqiu and occasionally employed civilian scholars. Eastern Han restoration reverted exclusively to eunuchs. Absorbed the duties of the abolished Qin-era Zhanshi (詹事), who previously outranked the Changqiu.

title_en: Assistant to the Grand Empress's Chamberlain

personnel_count.notes: Staffed by a eunuch

responsibilities: Assist the Grand Empress's Chamberlain in administrative management of the Empress's palace
historical_notes: Rank set at 600 Shi per statutory rules for assistants to actual 2000 Shi directors.

personnel_count.notes: Staffed by a eunuch

responsibilities: Manage the horses, carriages, and transport of the Empress
historical_notes: Originally titled Taipu (太僕) with a rank of 2000 Shi. During the Eastern Han restoration, the prefix 'Tai' was dropped and the rank demoted to 1000 Shi to subordinate it to the Dachangqiu.

personnel_count.notes: Staffed by a eunuch; oversees 3 subordinate Receptionists (中宮謁者) ranked at 400 Shi

responsibilities: Receive, process, and report formal documents and memorials submitted to the Empress's palace
historical_notes: Functions as the primary communications gatekeeper for the inner palace.

personnel_count.notes: Staffed by eunuchs

responsibilities: Manage internal palace documentation, correspondence, and secretarial records
historical_notes: Mirrors the executive function of the imperial Masters of Writing (尚書) within the inner apartments.

title_en: Prefect of the Empress's Private Treasury

personnel_count.notes: Staffed by a eunuch; assisted by 1 Cheng (300 Shi per meta-rules)

responsibilities: Manage stored valuables, currency, and silk textiles of the inner palace
Supervise tailoring of garments, mending of bedding, and laundry operations
historical_notes: Serves as the fiscal and material quartermaster for the Empress's household.

title_en: Prefect of the Empress's Long Lane

personnel_count.notes: Staffed by a eunuch; assisted by 1 Cheng

responsibilities: Maintain order, discipline, and registry of female palace attendants and junior concubines
historical_notes: The 'Long Lane' (永巷) historically designated the residential quarters and detention areas for court women.

title_en: Supervisor of the Empress's Yellow Gate Attendants-in-Waiting

personnel_count.notes: Staffed by a eunuch

responsibilities: Command and supervise the supernumerary eunuch attendants acting as guards and messengers in the inner palace
historical_notes: Puye (僕射) denotes a chief military or disciplinary overseer within Han domestic agencies.

title_en: Prefect of the Empress's Palace Office

personnel_count.notes: Staffed by a eunuch; oversees 6 Female Riders (女騎), 1 Assistant (丞), and 1 Passageway Assistant (復道丞)

responsibilities: Maintain physical cleanliness and maintenance of the Empress's quarters
Keep precise logs of the Emperor's visits to the inner apartments
Supervise the covered elevated passageways (閣道) connecting palace complexes via the Fudao Cheng
historical_notes: Critical recording agency determining imperial lineage legitimacy through imperial visitation logs.

personnel_count.notes: Staffed by a eunuch

responsibilities: Manage pharmaceutical supplies, medical compounds, and health treatments for the Empress
historical_notes: Dedicated inner-palace medical officer distinct from the Grand Physician's public bureau.

title_en: Junior Tutor to the Crown Prince

personnel_count.notes: Executive head of the Crown Prince's household

responsibilities: Assist and guide the Crown Prince
Exercise supreme administrative jurisdiction over all officials assigned to the Crown Prince's palace
historical_notes: When an Emperor ascends the throne prior to designating a new Crown Prince, all subordinate officials under this office are dismissed except the Sherin (舍人), who temporarily transfer to the Shaofu (Minister Steward).

title_en: Prefect of the Crown Prince's Timekeeping
responsibilities: Manage the rotational night watch and guard duty schedules of the Shuzi and Sherin
Execute disciplinary and household management duties mirroring the Superintendent of the Imperial Household (光祿)
historical_notes: The title derives from sounding the night watches (更) for the princely guard units.

personnel_count.notes: No fixed quota (無員)

responsibilities: Serve as attendants-in-waiting and ceremonial escorts to the Crown Prince
historical_notes: Structured functionally analogous to the Gentlemen-of-the-Household (中郎) of the Three Corps.

personnel_count.notes: No fixed quota (無員)

responsibilities: Perform rotational overnight security and bodyguard duties (更直宿衛)
historical_notes: Structured functionally analogous to the Gentlemen (郎中) of the Three Corps. Only personnel retained when a Crown Prince ascends the throne.

title_en: Household Steward to the Crown Prince
responsibilities: Manage granary stores, cereal supplies, and dietary provisions for the princely household
Execute fiscal and domestic responsibilities mirroring the Minister of Finance (司農) and Minister Steward (少府)
historical_notes: Primary domestic quartermaster for the heir apparent.

title_en: Prefect of the Crown Prince's Granaries
responsibilities: Supervise the storage and disbursement of grain and cereals
historical_notes: Subordinate specialist operating under the domestic purview of the Household Steward.

title_en: Prefect of the Crown Prince's Kitchens
responsibilities: Manage the preparation and service of food and beverages for the Crown Prince
historical_notes: Culinary directorate responsible for safeguarding the heir against dietary poisoning.

responsibilities: Manage carriages, chariots, and horses for the heir apparent
Execute duties mirroring the Grand Coachman (太僕)
historical_notes: Maintains the transport infrastructure and stables of the Eastern Palace.

title_en: Chief of the Crown Prince's Stables
responsibilities: Supervise daily husbandry, fodder, and maintenance of princely horses and vehicles
historical_notes: Subordinate stablemaster assisting the Crown Prince's Coachman.

title_en: Grand Master of the Crown Prince's Gate

personnel_count.notes: Unspecified quota

responsibilities: Supervise security and access control at the gates of the Crown Prince's palace
historical_notes: Status equivalent to a Household General (郎將). Previously assisted by Left and Right Gate Commanders (左右戶將) overseeing duty guards; these subordinate posts were abolished after the Jianwu period.

title_en: Palace Cadets to the Crown Prince
responsibilities: Serve as confidential inner attendants and advisors to the Crown Prince
historical_notes: Functional status and intimacy mirror the Palace Attendants (侍中) serving the Emperor.

personnel_count.notes: Old annotation specifies 16 personnel

responsibilities: Lead the vanguard during royal excursions to clear the way and display ceremonial dignity
Perform reception and protocol duties mirroring imperial Receptionists (謁者)
historical_notes: The title literally means 'washing horses' but institutionally designates the equestrian vanguard.

title_en: Palace Shield to the Crown Prince
responsibilities: Manage perimeter sentries and direct guard patrols around the princely residential complex
historical_notes: Security officer dedicated to the immediate physical circumference of the heir.

title_en: Commander of the Crown Prince's Guards
responsibilities: Command the armed sentries stationed at the entry portals of the Crown Prince's palace
historical_notes: Direct tactical commander of the gate sentry detachments.

personnel_count.notes: Assisted by 1 Cheng ranked at 600 Shi

responsibilities: Construct and repair ancestral temples, state halls (路寢), palace buildings, and imperial mausoleums
Supervise civil engineering works in timber and earthwork
Cultivate and plant roadside arboriculture, specifically paulownia and catalpa trees
historical_notes: Inherited from the Qin Dynasty post of Jiangzuo Shaofu (將作少府). Renamed Jiangzuo Dajiang by Emperor Jing of the Western Han.

title_en: Prefect of the Left Construction Yard

personnel_count.notes: Assisted by 1 Cheng (300 Shi per meta-rules)

responsibilities: Manage and direct the left division of state construction laborers and artisans
historical_notes: One of two primary operational labor directorates under the Grand Architect.

title_en: Prefect of the Right Construction Yard

personnel_count.notes: Assisted by 1 Cheng (300 Shi per meta-rules)

responsibilities: Manage and direct the right division of state construction laborers and artisans
historical_notes: Sister agency to the Left Yard, dividing metropolitan engineering workloads.

personnel_count.notes: Assisted by a Cheng ranked at equivalent 600 Shi per meta-rules

responsibilities: Maintain supreme military security and access control over the 12 metropolitan city gates of Luoyang
historical_notes: Centralized metropolitan defense command guarding the capital perimeter wall.

responsibilities: Exercise tactical command over military guard detachments stationed at the capital gates
historical_notes: Primary executive military officer under the Colonel of the City Gates.

personnel_count.notes: Exactly one Captain assigned to each of the 12 gates

responsibilities: Direct daily guard operations, opening/closing protocols, and inspections at assigned city portals
historical_notes: Oversees the 12 gates of Luoyang: Pingcheng (Main South), Shangxi, Yong, Guangyang, Jin, Xiaoyuan, Kaiyang, Mao, Zhongdong, Shangdong, Gu, and Xia. The Northern Palace Gate (北宮門) was administratively detached under the Commandant of the Palace Guards (衛尉).

responsibilities: Monitor, audit, and supervise the military administration of the Five Regimental Camps of the Northern Army
historical_notes: Western Han originally established the Zhonglei Xiaowei (Colonel of the Central Rampart) to govern Northern Army fortifications, alongside specialized Huqi and Huben Colonels created by Emperor Wu. During the Eastern Han restoration, the Zhonglei office was abolished and replaced by this low-ranking Supervisor to prevent military consolidation. Huqi cavalry were merged into the Changshui regiment; Huben light chariots were absorbed by the Shesheng regiment.

responsibilities: Command elite garrison cavalry units assigned to capital guard duties
historical_notes: First of the Five Regimental Colonels forming the standing metropolitan field army.

responsibilities: Assist the Colonel in tactical command and regimental administration
historical_notes: Standard regimental executive officer.

responsibilities: Command elite picked cavalry units safeguarding the royal capital
historical_notes: Second regimental command of the Northern Army.

responsibilities: Assist the Colonel in tactical command and regimental administration
historical_notes: Standard regimental executive officer.

responsibilities: Command standing metropolitan infantry regiments
historical_notes: Third regimental command of the Northern Army.

responsibilities: Assist the Colonel in tactical command and regimental administration
historical_notes: Standard regimental executive officer.

responsibilities: Command standing auxiliary cavalry regiments, specifically incorporating Wuhuan mounted archers
historical_notes: Fourth regimental command. Absorbed the Western Han Huqi (Barbarian Cavalry) regiment during Eastern Han reforms.

responsibilities: Assist the Colonel in regular cavalry administration
historical_notes: Standard regimental executive officer.

responsibilities: Command and administer incorporated Wuhuan auxiliary cavalry detachments
historical_notes: Specialized auxiliary officer attached to the Changshui regiment following the abolition of the independent Huqi Colonel.

title_en: Colonel of Archers who Fire at a Sound
responsibilities: Command elite metropolitan archer and crossbow regiments
historical_notes: Fifth regimental command. The title implies marksmen capable of shooting accurately by sound in darkness. Absorbed the Western Han Huben light chariot tactical units during restoration reforms.

title_en: Major of Archers who Fire at a Sound
responsibilities: Assist the Colonel in tactical command and archery training
historical_notes: Standard regimental executive officer.

personnel_count.notes: Governs 7 central commanderies

responsibilities: Hold supervisory and censoratorial jurisdiction over central officials and capital commanderies
Investigate, impeach, and prosecute illegal activities committed by high officials and royal kin
historical_notes: Originally established by Emperor Wu holding an imperial warrant (持節). Warrant stripped by Emperor Yuan; office abolished by Emperor Cheng. Restored during Jianwu period taking territorial jurisdiction over the central province (Sili).

personnel_count.notes: Appointed directly by provincial administration (自辟除); uniformly ranked at 100 Shi

responsibilities: Investigate official corruption across specialized bureaus (Capital, Merit, Mounted Escort, Accounts, Military)
Inspect provincial commandery documents and impeach unlawful administrators
historical_notes: Includes specialized officers: Duguan (Capital offenses), Gongcao (Personnel), Biejia (Tour escort), Bucao (Finance), Bingcao (Military), and regional Commandery Inspectors.

personnel_count.notes: Filled by seconded local commandery clerks on one-year annual rotations (歲滿一更)

responsibilities: Maintain office registries, manage documentation, and supervise specialized institutional tests (Confucian Classics, seasonal rituals, statutes)
historical_notes: Consists of sub-clerical specialists including Gate Chiefs (門亭長), Merit Clerks (門功曹書佐), Classicists (孝經師), Ritualists (月令師), Legalists (律令師), and Commandery Archivists (典郡書佐).

personnel_count.notes: Granted special court audience privileges (特奉朝請)

responsibilities: Administer the metropolitan capital commandery of Luoyang and surrounding districts
historical_notes: During the Western Han, the capital Chang'an was governed by the Three Fu (Jingzhao, Zuofengyi, Youfufeng) ranked at Zhong 2000 Shi. When the Eastern Han moved the capital to Luoyang, Henan Commandery was upgraded to be administered by a Yin (Governor). The Three Fu retained their titles due to imperial tombs but suffered rank reductions.
